examples.title: Matrix Chain Multiplication (2D DP)
examples.description: Finding optimal parenthesization for matrix chain multiplication to minimize scalar multiplications
examples.code:
def matrix_chain_order(p):
    n = len(p) - 1
    # dp[i][j] = minimum scalar multiplications for matrices Ai...Aj
    dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
    
    # length of chain
    for length in range(2, n + 1):
        for i in range(n - length + 1):
            j = i + length - 1
            dp[i][j] = float('inf')
            
            # try all split points
            for k in range(i, j):
                cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k + 1][j] + p[i] * p[k + 1] * p[j + 1]
                dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], cost)
    
    return dp[0][n - 1]

# Example: Matrices A(10x30), B(30x5), C(5x60)
dimensions = [10, 30, 5, 60]
result = matrix_chain_order(dimensions)
print(f"Minimum scalar multiplications: {result}")

# Traceback to find optimal parenthesization
def print_optimal_parens(p, dp, i, j):
    if i == j:
        return f"M{i+1}"
    return f"({print_optimal_parens(p, dp, i, (i+j)//2)} x {print_optimal_parens(p, dp, (i+j)//2+1, j)})"

# Reconstruct solution
n = len(p) - 1
dp = [[0] * n for _ in range(n)]
for length in range(2, n + 1):
    for i in range(n - length + 1):
        j = i + length - 1
        dp[i][j] = float('inf')
        for k in range(i, j):
            cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]
            if cost < dp[i][j]:
                dp[i][j] = cost

print(f"Optimal parenthesization: {print_optimal_parens(p, dp, 0, n-1)}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates 2D DP where we build solutions for subproblems of increasing length. We compute dp[i][j] representing minimum cost to multiply matrices Ai through Aj. For each possible split point k, we calculate cost = cost(left) + cost(right) + cost of multiplying resultant matrices. Time complexity O(n³) is improved from exponential brute force.

examples.title: Edit Distance with Space Optimization
examples.description: Computing Levenshtein distance with both standard 2D and space-optimized 1D approaches
examples.explanation: This example shows classic edit distance DP where dp[i][j] represents minimum operations to convert s1[0:i] to s2[0:j]. The optimized version uses only two rows instead of full 2D table, reducing space from O(mn) to O(n). The traceback version demonstrates how to reconstruct actual operations, essential for understanding algorithm behavior.

examples.title: Bitmask DP - Traveling Salesman Problem
examples.description: Solving TSP using bitmask dynamic programming with state compression
examples.code:
import sys

def tsp_bitmask(dist, start=0):
    n = len(dist)
    # dp[mask][i] = minimum cost to visit cities in 'mask' ending at city i
    # mask includes visited cities
    dp = [[float('inf')] * n for _ in range(1 << n)]
    dp[1 << start][start] = 0
    
    # Iterate through all masks
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        # Skip if start city not in mask (invalid state)
        if not (mask & (1 << start)):
            continue
        
        # Try to go from current city to next unvisited city
        for last in range(n):
            if dp[mask][last] == float('inf'):
                continue
            
            # Try to go to next city
            for next_city in range(n):
                if mask & (1 << next_city):  # already visited
                    continue
                
                new_mask = mask | (1 << next_city)
                new_cost = dp[mask][last] + dist[last][next_city]
                dp[new_mask][next_city] = min(dp[new_mask][next_city], new_cost)
    
    # Find minimum cost to return to start
    full_mask = (1 << n) - 1
    result = min(dp[full_mask][i] + dist[i][start] for i in range(n))
    
    return result

# Also return the path
def tsp_with_path(dist, start=0):
    n = len(dist)
    dp = [[float('inf')] * n for _ in range(1 << n)]
    parent = [[-1] * n for _ in range(1 << n)]
    
    dp[1 << start][start] = 0
    
    for mask in range(1 << n):
        if not (mask & (1 << start)):
            continue
        
        for last in range(n):
            if dp[mask][last] == float('inf'):
                continue
            
            for next_city in range(n):
                if mask & (1 << next_city):
                    continue
                
                new_mask = mask | (1 << next_city)
                new_cost = dp[mask][last] + dist[last][next_city]
                
                if new_cost < dp[new_mask][next_city]:
                    dp[new_mask][next_city] = new_cost
                    parent[new_mask][next_city] = last
    
    full_mask = (1 << n) - 1
    min_cost = float('inf')
    end_city = -1
    
    for i in range(n):
        cost = dp[full_mask][i] + dist[i][start]
        if cost < min_cost:
            min_cost = cost
            end_city = i
    
    # Reconstruct path
    path = []
    mask = full_mask
    curr = end_city
    
    while curr != -1:
        path.append(curr)
        prev = parent[mask][curr]
        mask = mask ^ (1 << curr)
        curr = prev
    
    path.reverse()
    path = [start] + path  # Add start at beginning
    
    return min_cost, path

# Example with 4 cities
dist_matrix = [
    [0, 10, 15, 20],
    [10, 0, 35, 25],
    [15, 35, 0, 30],
    [20, 25, 30, 0]
]

print("Traveling Salesman Problem - Bitmask DP")
print(f"Distance matrix: {dist_matrix[0]}")
print(f"Minimum tour cost: {tsp_bitmask(dist_matrix)}")

cost, path = tsp_with_path(dist_matrix)
print(f"Optimal path: {[chr(65 + c) for c in path]}")  # Convert to letters
print(f"Total cost: {cost}")

# Show state progression for smaller example
def tsp_debug(dist, start=0):
    n = len(dist)
    dp = [[float('inf')] * n for _ in range(1 << n)]
    dp[1 << start][start] = 0
    
    print(f"\nState progression (n={n}):")
    for mask in range(1, 1 << n):
        if mask & (1 << start):
            for last in range(n):
                if dp[mask][last] < float('inf'):
                    print(f"  Mask {bin(mask):>5}, Last={last}: cost={dp[mask][last]}")

tsp_debug(dist_matrix)
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates bitmask DP (also called state compression DP) for the Traveling Salesman Problem. Key insight: use bitmask to represent subsets of visited cities - if bit i is set, city i has been visited. State dp[mask][i] stores minimum cost to visit exactly the cities in 'mask' ending at city i. This reduces exponential 2^n brute force to O(n² × 2^n). The parent array enables path reconstruction. Essential for competitive programming and optimization problems.
